   
# **🕵️‍♂️ TryHackMe Write-up: Tcpdump - The Basics**  
**Author:** AnonymousD  
 **Date:** March 2026  
 **Category:** Network Forensics / Packet Analysis  
## **📌 Introduction**  
Understanding how data moves across a network is a fundamental skill for any Cybersecurity professional. In this lab, I explored **Tcpdump**, a powerful command-line packet analyzer. This report documents my methodology and the solutions to the challenges presented in the TryHackMe "Tcpdump: The Basics" room.  
## **🛠 Step 1: Environment Setup**  
Before sniffing traffic, we must identify the active network interface.  
user@AnonymousD:~$ ip a s  
   
**Observation:** The primary interface is ens5 with the IP address 10.128.148.44.  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
## **🔍 Task-by-Task Analysis & Solutions**  
### **1️⃣ Basic Capture & Output**  
The first step was learning how to capture live traffic and limit the output to avoid being overwhelmed by data.  
user@AnonymousD:~$ sudo tcpdump -i ens5 -c 10 -n  
   
- -i ens5: Selects the specific interface.  
- -c 10: Limits the capture to 10 packets.  
- -n: Disables DNS resolution for faster output.  
### **2️⃣ Filtering by Protocol (ICMP, ARP, & DNS)**  
Real-world analysis requires filtering out the "noise" to find specific protocols. For these tasks, I analyzed the provided traffic.pcap file.  
**Q1: How many packets in traffic.pcap use the ICMP protocol?**  
user@AnonymousD:~$ tcpdump -r traffic.pcap icmp -n | wc -l  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
**Solution:** 26  
 **Method:** Filtered for ICMP and piped to wc -l to count the lines.  
**Q2: What is the IP address of the host that asked for the MAC address of 192.168.124.137?**  
user@AnonymousD:~$ tcpdump -r traffic.pcap arp -n  
07:18:29.940761 ARP, Request who-has 192.168.124.137 tell 192.168.124.148  
   
**Solution:** 192.168.124.148  
 **Method:** In an ARP request, the "tell" IP is the source host looking for the MAC address.  
**Q3: What hostname (subdomain) appears in the first DNS query?**  
user@AnonymousD:~$ tcpdump -r traffic.pcap port 53 -n -c 1  
07:18:24.058626 IP 192.168.124.137.33672 > 192.168.124.1.53: 39913+ A? mirrors.rockylinux.org.  
   
**Solution:** mirrors.rockylinux.org  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
### **3️⃣ Advanced Filtering (TCP Flags & Packet Size)**  
Diving deeper into the packet headers to identify specific behaviors.  
**Q1: How many packets have only the TCP Reset (RST) flag set?**  
user@AnonymousD:~$ tcpdump -r traffic.pcap "tcp[tcpflags] == tcp-rst" | wc -l  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
**Solution:** 57  
 **Method:** Used a specific flag filter to catch reset packets, often indicating blocked or aborted connections.  
**Q2: What is the IP address of the host that sent packets larger than 15000 bytes?**  
user@AnonymousD:~$ tcpdump -r traffic.pcap greater 15000 -n  
07:18:24.967023 IP 185.117.80.53.80 > 192.168.124.137.60518: length 20820  
   
**Solution:** 185.117.80.53  
 **Method:** Used the greater primitive to quickly find large data transfers.  
### **4️⃣ Link-Level Headers (MAC Addresses)**  
To see the physical hardware address, we must display the link-level header.  
**Question:** What is the MAC address of the host that sent an ARP request?  
user@AnonymousD:~$ tcpdump -r traffic.pcap -e arp -n  
07:18:29.940761 52:54:00:23:60:2b > ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff, ethertype ARP, length 42...  
   
**Solution:** 52:54:00:23:60:2b  
 **Method:** The -e flag reveals the Source MAC address (the first one listed).  
## **💡 Summary Cheat Sheet**  
| | |  
|-|-|  
| **Flag** | **Description** |   
| -i | Select Interface |   
| -w | Write capture to a .pcap file |   
| -r | Read from a .pcap file |   
| -e | Display Link-level (MAC) headers |   
| -X | Display data in Hex and ASCII |   
| -v | Verbose output (more details) |   
## **🏁 Conclusion**  
Tcpdump is the "Swiss Army Knife" of network analysis. This lab reinforced my ability to dissect network traffic from **Layer 2 (Data Link)** through  **Layer 4 (Transport)**. These skills are essential for  **incident response** and  **traffic auditing**.  
Stay curious. Stay secure.  
#CyberSecurity #Networking #Tcpdump #TryHackMe #Anonymousd
